coredata.dc:title: A Novel method for the preparation of fluorescent C60 poly(amino acid) composites and their biological imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Dazhuang
Liu, Meiying
Huang, Qiang
Chen, Junyu
Huang, Hongye
Deng, Fengjie
Tian, Jianwen
Wen, Yuanqing
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Recently, fullerene (C60) and its derivatives have been widely explored for many applications owing to their enriched physical and chemical properties. Specifically, the synthesis and biomedical applications of fluorescent C60 have been extensively investigated previously. However, the preparation of polymer-functionalized fluorescent C60 has not been reported thus far. In this study, water-dispersible fluorescent C60 polymer composites were successfully synthesized through the combination of the thiol-ene click reaction and subsequent ring-opening polymerization. First, 2-aminoethanethiol was introduced on the surface of C60 by the thiol-ene click reaction. The surface of amino group-functionalized C60 (C60-NH2) was further modified with poly(amino acid)s via ring-open polymerization of GluEG N-carboxyanhydrides (NCAs). The morphology, functional groups, optical properties and biocompatibility were examined by a number of characterization equipment and assays in detail. We demonstrated that the resultant fluorescent C60 poly(amino acid) (C60-GluEG) composites have a small size (about 5 nm), high water dispersibility, intense fluorescence and high photostability. Cell viability results implied that the C60-GluEG composites possess low cytotoxicity. Moreover, these C60-GluEG composites can easily penetrate into live cells, indicating their great potential for biological imaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent C60 nanoparticles
Thiol-ene click
N-carboxyanhydrides
Biological imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 1H NMR spectra of C60-NH2 and C60-GluEG. The 1H NMR spectra were obtained using CDCl3 and d6-DMSO as the solvents.
FT-IR spectra of C60, C60-NH2 and C60-GluEG.
TGA curves of C60, C60-NH2 and C60-GluEG.

Room temperature emission spectrum of C60-GluEG composites dispersed in water with an excitation wavelength at 460 nm. Inset shows a fluorescence photograph of the nanoparticle suspension taken at 365 nm UV light.
Cell viability evaluation of C60-GluEG composites towards HeLa cells based on the CCK-8 assay. The cells were incubated with 10–120 μg mL−1 nanoparticles for 12 and 24 h.
CLSM images of HeLa cells after incubation with 40 μg mL−1 C60-GluEG composites for 3 h. (A) Excited with 458 nm, (B) bright field, (C) merged image of A and B. Scale bar = 20 μm.
Schematic showing the experimental procedure for the synthesis of polypeptide-polymer-functionalized fluorescent C60 (C60-GluEG).

head.ce:title: A Novel method for the preparation of fluorescent C60 poly(amino acid) composites and their biological imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, fullerene (C60) and its derivatives have been widely explored for many applications owing to their enriched physical and chemical properties. Specifically, the synthesis and biomedical applications of fluorescent C60 have been extensively investigated previously. However, the preparation of polymer-functionalized fluorescent C60 has not been reported thus far. In this study, water-dispersible fluorescent C60 polymer composites were successfully synthesized through the combination of the thiol-ene click reaction and subsequent ring-opening polymerization. First, 2-aminoethanethiol was introduced on the surface of C60 by the thiol-ene click reaction. The surface of amino group-functionalized C60 (C60-NH2) was further modified with poly(amino acid)s via ring-open polymerization of GluEG N-carboxyanhydrides (NCAs). The morphology, functional groups, optical properties and biocompatibility were examined by a number of characterization equipment and assays in detail. We demonstrated that the resultant fluorescent C60 poly(amino acid) (C60-GluEG) composites have a small size (about 5 nm), high water dispersibility, intense fluorescence and high photostability. Cell viability results implied that the C60-GluEG composites possess low cytotoxicity. Moreover, these C60-GluEG composites can easily penetrate into live cells, indicating their great potential for biological imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The past few years have witnessed the rapid development of fluorescent nanoparticles in biomedicine for use as probes in biological sensing, imaging and labelling [1–3]. Significant efforts have been made in the fabrication of fluorescent nanoparticles including quantum dots (QDs), lanthanide ion-doped nanomaterials and fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) [4–8]. Recently, carbon-based fluorescent nanoparticles have been developed as ideal alternatives in biomedical applications because they have the advantages of remarkable optical properties, water dispersibility and biocompatibility [9,10]. Up to now, various nanostructured carbon materials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, fullerene (C60) and nanodiamonds have been developed [11–13]. As the third allotrope of carbon after diamond and graphite, C60 with precisely controllable size and structure has attracted much attention for applications in optoelectronic devices, organic photovoltaics, catalysis and drug delivery systems owing to its unique structural, optical, and electrical properties [14–17]. However, the fluorescence of C60 is very weak with a quantum yield on the order of 10−4 in toluene [18]. The high degree of symmetry in the closed-shell electronic configuration results in low-lying electronic transitions in C60 only weakly being allowed. Besides, the low solubility and easy aggregation of C60 further limit their applications in biomedicine. Several methods have been used to make C60 water soluble by functionalizing the C60 cage with polymers, ceramics and metals [19–22]. In addition, some of these C60 derivatives are structurally perturbed fullerene molecules and provide a general way to understand the photophysical behaviour in comparison with pure C60. Significant progress has been made recently in the preparation and biomedical applications of these C60 derivatives [23–28]. However, only very limited reports have demonstrated the fabrication of biodegradable polymer-functionalized fluorescent C60.

There have been many efforts to construct biodegradable polymers. Several systems based on polyesters, poly(amino ester)s and poly(amino acid)s have been developed [29–31]. Among them, synthetic polypeptides (i.e., poly(amino acids)) are of great attractiveness because of their high potential in biotechnology and biomedical applications such as drug delivery, tissue engineering and therapeutics [29–31]. The synthesis of poly(amino acids) from controlled ring-open polymerization of (ROP) of α-amino acid NCAs is perhaps the most established and convenient method. Peptide polymers have many advantages over conventional polymers because polypeptides have a stable secondary structure that contributes to the self-assembling character of polypeptide chains due to cooperative hydrogen bonds, π-π stacking and van der Waals forces [32]. At present, various functional polypeptides based on natural amino acids have been synthesized. The abundant variety of naturally available amino acid monomers has allowed researchers to fabricate various polypeptides with different functions. Furthermore, the properties of polypeptides can be tailored by adjusting the functional groups in the amino acid side chain. Considering their promising application in biomedicine, it is of significance to use biocompatible polypeptides to functionalize C60.

The conjugation of biodegradable polypeptides as the matrix, along with the fabrication of monodisperse organic-inorganic hybrid C60 complexes under mild conditions, generates the high potential of polypeptide-polymer-conjugated C60 in biomedical applications. Herein, we use ethylene glycol side chain-modified glutamic acid (GluEG) NCAs to functionalize C60 (Scheme 1 ). In this study, 2-aminoethanethiol was first introduced on the surface of C60 via the thiol-ene click reaction. Free amine groups could be used to initiate the polymerization of GluEG NCA monomer for the fabrication of biodegradable C60 with excellent water dispersibility. These prepared fluorescent C60 (C60-GluEG) composites show visible emission, and the secondary structure of GluEG may significantly expand their applications in nanotechnology.

All of the reagents and solvents were purchased from commercial sources and used directly without further purification. Major materials such as C60, l-glutamic acid, ethylene glycol monomethylether, dicumyl peroxide and 2-aminoethanethiol hydrochloride were purchased from Aladdin. Sodium hydroxide and sulfuric acid were obtained from Heowns (Tianjin, China). The GluEG NCA monomer was prepared according to previous reports [33].

1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance-400 spectrometer with CDCl3 d6-DMSO as the solvent. The synthetic C60 derivative was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) using KBr pellets. FT-IR spectra of C60, C60-NH2 and C60-GluEG were obtained from a Nicolet 5700 (Thermo Nicolet corporation) with a resolution of 2 cm−1. The morphology and size of C60-GluEG composites were measured using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a Hitachi 7650B microscope operated at 80 kV, and the TEM specimens were obtained by placing a drop of the nanoparticle suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid. Fluorescence spectra were measured using an F-4500 FL spectrophotometer with a slit width of 5 nm for the emission of C60-GluEG composites in water.

A mixture of C60 (300 mg) and DMF (100 mL) was sonicated for 1 h. Subsequently, 2-aminoethanethiol hydrochloride (20 mmol, 2.27 g) and dicumyl peroxide (10 mmol, 2.7 g) were added and stirred at 120 °C for 48 h under nitrogen conditions. 2-Aminoethanethiol could be introduced on the surface of C60 via the thiol-ene click reaction. After the reaction was completed, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, and a moderate amount of sodium hydroxide solution was added. Finally, the solution containing crude product (C60-NH2) was collected in a dialysis tube and dialysed with ethanol and deionized water three times.

The amino groups on the C60 provide a platform for further functionalizing C60 with biodegradable polymers. The amino groups acted as initiators for grafting GluEG NCA monomers on the fluorescent C60 nanoparticles through ring-opening polymerization. Briefly, C60-NH2 (100 mg) was dissolved in 20 mL of anhydrous THF, and then 100 mg of GluEG NCAs was added. The mixture was deaerated with N2 for 10 min and stirred at 40 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was completed, the product was collected by dialysis and washed with deionized water 3 times to remove any unreacted chemicals.

The cell viability of C60-GluEG composites on HeLa cells was evaluated by the cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay based on our previous works [34–37]. Briefly, cells were seeded in 96-well microplates at a concentration of 5 × 104 cells mL−1 in 160 μL of respective media containing 10% foetal bovine serum (FBS). After cell attachment for 24 h, the cells were incubated with 10, 20, 40, 80 and 120 μg mL−1 concentrations for 12 and 24 h. The nanoparticles were then removed, and the cells were washed with PBS three times. Next, 10 μL of CCK-8 dye and 100 μL of DMEM cell culture medium were added to each well and incubated for another 2 h at 37 °C. The plates were then analysed with a microplate reader (Victor III, Perkin-Elmer). Measurements of dye absorbance were carried out at 450 nm, with the reference wavelength at 620 nm. The values were proportional to the number of live cells. The percent reduction of WST was compared to the control (cells not exposed to C60-GluEG composites), which represented 100% WST reduction. Three replicate wells were used for each control and test concentrations per microplate, and the experiment was repeated three times.

The cell uptake behaviour of C60-GluEG composites was evaluated by confocal microscopic imaging. Briefly, HeLa cells were seeded in a glass-bottom dish at a density of 1 × 105 cells per dish. On the day of treatment, the cells were incubated with C60-GluEG composites at a concentration of 40 μg mL−1 for 3 h at 37 °C. After that, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the un-internalized C60-GluEG composites and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min at room temperature. Cell images were taken with a Zeiss 710 3-channel Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM) (Zeiss, Germany) with an excitation wavelength of 458 nm.

Recently, C60 and C60 derivatives have been widely explored in many different fields including biomedicine owing to their high demands of physical and chemical properties. In this article, polypeptide-modified fluorescent C60 composites were prepared via the combination of the thiol-ene click reaction and ring-opening polymerization of GluEG NCAs. First, 2-aminoethanethiol was conjugated on the surface of C60 via the thiol-ene click reaction, and these functional groups provide a platform for further modification of C60. The successful synthesis of C60-NH2 and polypeptide-functionalized fullerene was evidenced by a series of characterization techniques. Fig. S1A displays the 1H NMR spectra of GluEG and GluEG NCAs. Some characteristic peaks belonging to the ethylene glycol monomethyl ether part were found in the GluEG sample. After GluEG was converted into the corresponding NCAs, new proton peaks at 7.5 and 4.4 ppm emerged (Fig. S1B). The side chain-modified GluEG NCAs can graft to the fullerene surface by the amino-initiated ring-open polymerization. Hence, the 1H NMR spectra of C60-NH2 and C60-GluEG were further investigated to identify the successful formation of polypeptide-functionalized fullerene composites. Fig. 1 shows the 1H NMR spectra of C60-NH2 and C60-GluEG by using CDCl3 and d6-DMSO as the solvents. The characteristic peak at 3.6 ppm that can be assigned to the EG moiety emerged in C60-GluEG, demonstrating the successful preparation of polypeptide-polymer-modified C60 derivatives.

The successful formation of C60-GluEG was also confirmed by FT-IR spectra. As displayed in Fig. 2 , the newly emerged double peaks at 3405 and 3311 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibration of the amino group in the sample of C60-NH2. In addition, the intense in-plane flexural vibration signal of amino at 1660 cm−1 was also observed, demonstrating the successful synthesis of C60-NH2 via the thiol-ene click reaction. After the polypeptides were conjugated on the C60 surface through amino-initiated ring-open polymerization of NCA monomers, some changes were observed compared with C60-NH2. For example, the double peaks contributed by the amino groups at 3405 and 3311 cm−1 disappeared and the stretching C-O vibration at 1073 cm−1 emerged. These changes illustrated that polypeptides were decorated on the fullerene surface successfully.

The TGA and DTA results provide more visual evidence for the surface functionalization of C60 via the polymerization of GluEG. The grafting amount of polymers was calculated based on the ratio of weight loss by TGA. Fig. 3 shows the TGA curves of C60 and C60 derivatives with temperature increasing from room temperature to 800 °C. Pure C60 had no weight loss when the temperature was lower than 500 °C but showed 100% weight loss when the temperature was up to 800 °C. Compared with pure C60, the weight loss of C60-NH2 was calculated to be about 55% when the temperature was heated to 570 °C, which was mainly due to 2-aminoethanethiol on the C60 surface. Additionally, there was another weight loss in the temperature range of 570–800 °C, which belonged to the decomposition of C60. After the reaction with GluEG monomers, C60-GluEG showed a two-stage weight loss at the temperature ranges of 200–500 °C and 500–800 °C. Similar results could be obtained from the DTA curves (Fig. S2). Both results indicated the successful fabrication of polypeptide-polymer-modified C60 derivatives.

The size and morphology of C60-GluEG were characterized by TEM. As displayed in Fig. 4. , C60-GluEG composites show relatively uniform spherical particles with a diameter of about 4.5 ± 1.2 nm. In addition, these particles were homogeneously dispersed and no obvious aggregates can be observed, demonstrating that C60-GluEG composites had excellent water dispersibility. This phenomenon suggested that polypeptides were successfully conjugated on the surface of C60, and the hydrophilic and biocompatible polypeptide-polymer matrix endowed C60 with super dispersity. Furthermore, the polypeptides were biodegradable, and such prepared C60-GluEG composites had advantages of high water dispersibility, low toxicity and excellent degradability, which show great potential in biomedical applications.

The fluorescent properties of C60-GluEG composites such as intensity and photostability play an important role in their biomedical applications. Fig. 5 shows the photoluminescence spectrum of C60-GluEG composites dispersed in aqueous solution with an excitation wavelength at 460 nm. The maximum emission wavelength of C60-GluEG composites was located at 540 nm. Meanwhile, a strong yellow luminescence could be observed under irradiation with 365 nm light (inset of Fig. 5). In addition, the photostability of C60-GluEG composites was further investigated. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the C60-GluEG suspension was further investigated. As displayed in Fig. S3, C60-GluEG composites were homogeneously dispersed (inset) and two major absorption peaks at 271 and 282 nm could be observed, which were different from the spectrum of the original C60. Besides, the entire absorption spectrum was elevated from 600 nm. The above results demonstrated that C60 derivatives were successfully prepared and showed excellent optical properties and water dispersibility. The effect of excitation wavelength on the fluorescence properties of C60-GluEG composites was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S4, the emission peaks were slowly red-shifted with the increase in excitation wavelength from 400 to 490 nm. The excitation wavelength-dependent phenomenon has also been observed for other fluorescent carbon dots [38,39]. The photostability of C60-GluEG composites was further examined to evaluate their biological imaging performance. As shown in Fig. S5, it was exciting to observe that the fluorescence intensity of C60-GluEG composites could remain stable during 2 h and 34 h of persistent irradiation with an ultraviolet lamp. The fluorescence reduction percentage was calculated to be about 2.5% after 2 h of irradiation. The above results demonstrated that the C60-GluEG composites possess good fluorescence property and are of great potential for biological imaging [40,41].

The biocompatibility was evaluated to determine the potential biomedical applications of C60-GluEG composites. The effect of C60-GluEG composites on HeLa cells was investigated after the cells were incubated with different concentrations of C60-GluEG composites, and the cell viability of C60-GluEG composites towards HeLa cells was determined by the cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. As shown in Fig. 6 , no obvious decrease in cell viability was observed when the cells were incubated with 10–120 μg mL−1 C60-GluEG composites for 12 and 24 h. Even when the particle concentration was up to 120 μg mL−1, the cell viability values were still greater than 90%. The cell viability results implied that C60-GluEG composites possessed excellent biocompatibility and negative toxicity towards the cells. Considering the high water dispersibility and photostability as well as the excellent biocompatibility of C60-GluEG composites, these C60-GluEG composites should be promising candidates for cell imaging application.

Based on the merits of polypeptide-functionalized C60-GluEG composites, including high water dispersibility, high photostability and excellent biocompatibility, the cell uptake behaviours of C60-GluEG composites were evaluated by CLSM observation. As shown in Fig. 7 , intense yellow fluorescence signals were observed at the cell location after incubation with 40 μg mL−1 C60-GluEG composites for 3 h, and no significant background interference was detected. Besides, many areas with relatively weak fluorescence signals were also observed. The above results demonstrated that such prepared C60-GluEG composites could enter into the HeLa cells and mainly accumulate in the cytoplasm. Fig. 7B shows the bright field CLSM images, and it can be seen that the cells were still attached on the plates and kept their normal morphology. This also confirmed the good biocompatibility of C60-GluEG composites. Furthermore, the merged CLSM image from Fig. 7A and B is displayed in Fig. 7C. It can be clearly observed that the fluorescent areas were overlapped with the location of the cells and that the cell centre is devoid of fluorescence. The cell uptake behaviour suggested that C60-GluEG composites can be used as probes for biological imaging applications.

In summary, water-dispersible and biocompatible fullerene-based nanoprobes were successfully prepared via the polymerization of GluEG NCAs. The structure of the fluorescent C60 nanoparticles was a 5-nm sphere with a solid core of aggregated C60 and polypeptide-polymer shell, which improved the solubility and biocompatibility of the nanoparticles. The successful preparation of C60-based probes was evidenced by a number of characterization techniques. These polypeptide-functionalized C60-GluEG showed excellent water dispersibility and photostability, which is important for their biomedical applications. In addition, biological evaluation results demonstrated the low cytotoxicity of C60-GluEG composites towards HeLa cells. These particles showed desired properties for bioimaging applications by virtue of high luminescence, remarkable photostability, nontoxicity and easy penetration into live cells. Compared with other carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, such C60 nanoparticles are expected to be more practicable in biomedical applications. Compared with previous methods, the strategy described in this article has the advantages of easy operation and high efficiency. In addition, many functional polymers containing sulfhydryl can be directly conjugated on the surface of C60 to prepare fluorescent C60 nanoprobes by the one-step thiol-ene click reaction. More importantly, this strategy can be largely extended for the modification of other carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene and nanodiamonds.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014) and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.01.085.
